question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the JSONStringify custom serialization to trigger a denial-of-service by sending a request that causes the staking rewards responseData to contain a deeply nested or circular structure, causing JSONStringify to hang or throw, preventing legitimate users from querying their pending rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that res.locals[responseBodyLabel] is set to the serialized response before next() is called, allowing a downstream middleware (responseCacheUpdateHandler) to read and cache a staking rewards response that was intended for a different account due to request context confusion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the header merge order in mergedHeaders to inject an ETag header via res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] that matches a cached staking rewards response for a different account, causing the server to return a 304 Not Modified response with the wrong account's reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the path-based cache-control header selection to force a longer cache TTL on the /api/v1/accounts/{id}/rewards endpoint by manipulating requestPathLabel, causing the response cache to serve outdated pending_reward values that do not reflect recent reward payouts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that req.ip is logged directly without sanitization (logger.info(`${req.ip} ...`)) to inject log-forging payloads via X-Forwarded-For headers, obscuring their enumeration of staking reward amounts in the server logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the elapsed time logging (Date.now() - startTime) to infer the number of staking reward records for an account based on response latency, enabling them to identify high-value accounts with large pending_reward balances without directly reading the reward amounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the staking rewards response includes the full reward history (amount, timestamp, account_id) without any access control check in responseHandler, allowing any user to query any account's complete staking reward transfer history by simply knowing the account ID?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the mergedHeaders construction to set a Vary header that causes the response cache to store separate entries for different Accept-Encoding values, allowing the attacker to bypass cache and always receive fresh staking reward data including real-time pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseHandler calls res.send() with the full responseData including all reward amounts, and there is no field-level access control in the middleware, to retrieve the complete staking reward transfer history for any account including the exact pending_reward tinybar amounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the path lookup (headers.path[path]) with a null or undefined requestPathLabel to fall back to req.route.path, and by crafting a request that matches a route with permissive cache headers, cause staking reward responses to be cached with a TTL that allows replay of stale reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseHandler does not strip or sanitize the links.next URL before emitting it in the Link header, allowing a server-side request forgery (SSRF) scenario where a crafted links.next value causes automated clients to make requests to internal endpoints that expose staking reward calculation internals?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the JSONStringify serialization of the staking rewards response to cause key enumeration of the StakingRewardTransferViewModel object, revealing internal fields (such as raw database column values) that are not intended to be exposed in the public API response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the response cache (populated after responseHandler calls next()) stores the full serialized staking rewards response including all reward amounts, and by poisoning the cache key with a crafted request, cause other users to receive the attacker's query results containing a different account's reward data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sampleRate logging bypass (Math.random() <= sampleRate) to perform a timing attack where they send thousands of requests to enumerate pending_reward values across accounts, with only a fraction being logged, making the enumeration attack statistically invisible in server logs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseHandler reads res.locals[responseHeadersLabel] without validation, and if a prior middleware sets this to an attacker-influenced value (e.g., via a crafted request header that gets reflected), inject arbitrary HTTP headers into the staking rewards response that manipulate client-side caching behavior?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pending_reward field is included in the account response (GET /api/v1/accounts/{id}) which goes through responseHandler, and by querying accounts in bulk via the list endpoint, enumerate pending_reward values for all staking accounts to identify high-value targets for social engineering or transaction timing attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Link header emission (res.set(LINK_NEXT_HEADER, linkNextHeaderValue(linksNext))) to perform a cache deception attack where the Link header causes a caching proxy to store the staking rewards response under a URL that the attacker controls, allowing them to serve poisoned reward data to other users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseHandler does not enforce any rate limiting on staking rewards queries, allowing them to enumerate the complete reward history for all accounts on the network by iterating through account IDs and collecting pending_reward and historical reward amounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the mergedHeaders spread to override the Content-Type header with a value that causes the response to be interpreted as a different media type by downstream proxies, leading to incorrect parsing of the staking rewards JSON response and potential data corruption in cached reward records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that res.locals.responseData.links?.next is accessed via optional chaining on line 42, meaning if responseData has a links property that is not null but has a non-null next value, the Link header is always set, allowing an attacker to confirm pagination state and infer the total number of reward records for an account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the staking rewards endpoint accepts account lookup by EVM address (0x-prefixed hex), and by computing the EVM address corresponding to a known Hedera account, query that account's staking reward history through responseHandler without knowing the native account ID format?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the path-based header configuration (headers.path[path]) to identify which API paths have different cache TTLs, and by targeting the staking rewards path with a longer TTL, cause the cached response to persist long enough that reward data from a previous staking period is served as current, misleading users about their actual pending_reward balance?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseHandler.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: responseHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseHandler serializes the entire responseData object via JSONStringify without field-level redaction, and if the staking rewards controller accidentally includes internal database fields (e.g., payer_account_id) in the view model, these fields are exposed in the response to any unauthenticated user?
